title: Section C: Soil Mechanics & Foundation Engineering

sections.title: Chapter 1: Soil Mechanics - Phase Relations & Index Properties

rows: INTRODUCTION & SCOPE
Soil mechanics studies the physical and engineering behavior of soil as a construction material. Applications include foundations, embankments, retaining structures, and pavements.
PHASE RELATIONS OF SOIL

Soil is a three-phase system: solid particles, water, and air. Key parameters:
• Total volume: V = V_s + V_w + V_a
• Void ratio: e = V_v / V_s
• Porosity: n = V_v / V
• Degree of saturation: S_r = V_w / V_v × 100%
WATER CONTENT (w)
w = W_w / W_s × 100%, indicates moisture in soil; affects compaction and strength.
UNIT WEIGHT & DENSITY

• Bulk density: ρ = W / V
• Dry density: ρ_d = W_s / V
• Saturated density: ρ_sat = W_sat / V
• Specific gravity: G_s = ρ_s / ρ_w
INDEX PROPERTIES

• Liquid Limit (LL): moisture content at which soil changes from plastic to liquid
• Plastic Limit (PL): moisture content at which soil changes from semi-solid to plastic
• Plasticity Index (PI) = LL - PL
• Shrinkage Limit (SL): moisture content below which soil does not shrink further
SOIL IDENTIFICATION & CLASSIFICATION

• Coarse-grained: gravel & sand; fine-grained: silt & clay
• USCS / IS:1498 classification based on grain size and Atterberg limits
• Determines suitability for construction & drainage behavior

sections.title: Important Formulas - Phase Relations & Index Properties

options.text: Moisture below which no further shrinkage occurs
Moisture at which soil becomes liquid
Moisture at which soil becomes plastic

rows: PRINCIPLE OF EFFECTIVE STRESS

Effective stress governs soil strength and deformation. Introduced by Terzaghi:
• Total stress (σ) = stress applied to soil
• Pore water pressure (u) = pressure of water in voids
• Effective stress (σ') = stress transmitted through soil skeleton
σ' = σ - u
• Controls settlement, shear strength, and consolidation behavior of soils
CAPILLARITY IN SOILS

Phenomenon of water rising in small pores due to surface tension.
• Capillary rise (h_c) = height water rises above groundwater
• Capillary pressure (p_c) = pressure causing water to rise
• Quick sand condition occurs when upward seepage equals or exceeds weight of soil particles
TOTAL PRESSURE & ELEVATION HEADS

• Total head (H) = Pressure head (h_p) + Elevation head (z) + Velocity head (v²/2g)
• Pressure head = u / γ_w
• Elevation head = height of point above datum
• Quick sand occurs if upward hydraulic gradient i ≥ i_critical = γ_sat / γ_w - 1
PERMEABILITY OF SOIL

Ability of soil to allow water flow.
• Hydraulic conductivity (k) depends on soil type, void ratio, and viscosity
• Flow governed by Darcy's law: Q = k i A
• Determination by: Constant head test (coarse soils), Falling head test (fine soils)
• Units: m/s

items.name: CAPILLARY RISE
items.expr: h_c = 0.15 / d (in meters, d in mm for fine sands) approx.

options.text: Water rises in small pores due to surface tension
Water flows downward due to gravity
Pressure head + Elevation head + Velocity head

rows: COMPACTION – DEFINITION & PURPOSE

Process of increasing soil density by mechanical means to improve engineering properties.
• Reduces void ratio, increases shear strength, decreases compressibility, improves stability.
• Affects bearing capacity, settlement, and permeability.
PROCESS OF COMPACTION

• Compaction achieved by rolling, tamping, vibrating, or kneading.
• Moisture content crucial – optimum water content (w_opt) gives maximum dry density (ρ_dmax).
• Dry and wet of optimum:
– Dry: Soil too stiff, cannot rearrange → low density
– Wet: Water lubricates particles but reduces friction → density decreases
COMPACTION THEORIES

• Proctor Test: Determines ρ_dmax and w_opt.
• Modified Proctor: Higher energy compaction → higher ρ_dmax.
• Maximum Dry Density (ρ_dmax) used in field control.
MOISTURE-DENSITY RELATIONSHIP

• Standard Compaction Curve: ρ_d vs w (moisture content)
• Degree of Compaction (DC):
DC = (ρ_d,field / ρ_d,max) × 100%
EFFECT OF COMPACTION ON SOIL BEHAVIOR

• Increases shear strength and bearing capacity.
• Reduces settlement and compressibility.
• Decreases permeability.
COMPRESSIBILITY OF SOIL

• Measure of soil deformation under load.
• Compressibility index (Cc) for normally consolidated soil: Cc = (e_0 - e) / log(σ'_2 / σ'_1)
• Recompression index (Cr) for overconsolidated soil.
• Settlement (S) = H × Δe / (1 + e_0)
CONSOLIDATION OF SOIL

• Time-dependent process of volume decrease due to expulsion of water from pores under load.
• Governed by Terzaghi's 1D consolidation theory.
• Primary consolidation: vertical compression of soil skeleton.
• Secondary consolidation: creep of soil particles.
KEY CONSOLIDATION PARAMETERS

• Coefficient of Consolidation (Cv): Rate of consolidation
• Degree of consolidation (U): % consolidation achieved at time t
• Time factor: T_v = Cv × t / H²

items.name: RECOMPRESSION INDEX
items.expr: Cr = (e_0 - e) / log(σ'_2 / σ'_1) for OC soils

options.text: Creep of soil particles after primary consolidation

rows: SHEAR STRENGTH OF SOIL – DEFINITION
Ability of soil to resist shear stress. Depends on cohesion (c), internal friction angle (φ), and normal stress (σ). Governs stability of foundations, slopes, and retaining walls.
MOHR'S CIRCLE OF STRESS

Graphical representation of stress state in soil.
• Principal stresses: σ₁ (major), σ₃ (minor)
• Shear stress (τ) vs normal stress (σ) plot
• Failure envelope: τ = c + σ tan φ
COMPONENTS OF SHEAR STRENGTH

• Cohesion (c): Soil particle attraction, independent of normal stress
• Angle of internal friction (φ): Resistance due to interparticle friction
• Total shear stress: τ = c + σ tan φ
DETERMINATION OF SHEAR STRENGTH PARAMETERS

• Direct Shear Test → Measures c and φ under controlled normal stress
• Triaxial Test → Measures strength under drained (CD, CU) or undrained (UU) conditions
• Unconfined Compression Test → Measures cohesion for saturated clays (c_u)
STRESS PATH CONCEPT
Shows the path of stress change during loading in σ-τ space. Useful in understanding failure mechanisms in soils.
SLOPE STABILITY

• Analysis of slopes to prevent failure.
• Factors affecting stability: Slope angle, soil type, cohesion, internal friction, water content, surcharge loads
• Types of slope failure: Translational, rotational, wedge, circular slip.
FACTOR OF SAFETY (FS)

Ratio of resisting forces to driving forces on slope.
• FS = Resisting Shear / Driving Shear
• FS > 1 → Stable, FS < 1 → Failure likely
STABILITY ANALYSIS METHODS

• Analytical methods: Infinite slope, Swedish Circle method, Bishop's method, Janbu method
• Empirical methods: Based on experience, charts, and field observation
KEY INFLUENCES

• Groundwater lowers FS due to pore pressure
• Vegetation can improve stability
• Loadings such as buildings, roads, or excavation affect slope stability

sections.title: Important Formulas - Shear Strength & Slope Stability

items.name: SLOPE FACTOR OF SAFETY (SIMPLIFIED)
items.expr: FS = c'/(γH sin θ cos θ) + tan φ'/tan θ

items.name: DRIVING SHEAR ON SLOPE
items.expr: τ_d = γH sin θ cos θ

items.name: EFFECTIVE STRESS
items.expr: σ' = σ - u (where u = pore water pressure)

options.text: Provides drained and undrained shear strength

sections.title: Chapter 5: Foundation Engineering - Site Investigation & Earth Pressure

rows: SITE INVESTIGATION - INTRODUCTION & SCOPE

Site investigation studies subsurface conditions to design safe foundations. It includes exploration, sampling, and in-situ testing. Determines soil profile, groundwater level, and engineering properties.
METHODS OF SOIL EXPLORATION

• Boring: Rotary, Auger, Wash boring; reveals soil profile.
• Sampling: Disturbed (structure destroyed, for classification), Undisturbed (structure preserved, for strength tests)
• Field Tests: Standard Penetration Test (SPT), Plate Load Test
SOIL SAMPLING & TYPES

• Disturbed Sample – soil structure destroyed; used for classification, moisture content
• Undisturbed Sample – structure preserved; used for strength, consolidation
FIELD PENETRATION TEST

• SPT: Number of blows per 30 cm penetration → N-value
• CPT: Cone penetration, measures tip resistance & sleeve friction
PLATE LOAD TEST
Determines in-situ bearing capacity & settlement under controlled load
NUMBER OF BOREHOLES & DEPTH
Depends on: Structure type, variability of soil, water table depth, and foundation type
TYPES OF EARTH PRESSURE

• Active: Soil tends to move away from wall
• Passive: Soil resists wall movement
• At-Rest: Wall prevented from moving; pressure intermediate
RANKINE'S THEORY

Simplified for cohesionless soil; wall smooth & vertical
• Active: σ_a = γ H K_a - 2c√K_a
• Passive: σ_p = γ H K_p + 2c√K_p
• K_a = tan²(45 - φ/2), K_p = tan²(45 + φ/2)
COULOMB'S THEORY
Considers wall friction (δ) and inclined backfill (β). Provides more realistic pressure than Rankine
EARTH RETAINING STRUCTURES
Types: Gravity, Cantilever, Counterfort, Sheet pile walls. Designed to resist lateral earth pressure safely
COFFER DAM
Temporary structure for retaining water/soil during construction. Types: Earthen, Sheet pile, Cellular

sections.title: Important Formulas - Site Investigation & Earth Pressure

items.name: PLATE LOAD TEST
items.expr: q_allow = P / A; Settlement measured by dial gauges

items.name: RANKINE COEFFICIENTS
items.expr: K_a = tan²(45 - φ/2), K_p = tan²(45 + φ/2)

sections.title: Chapter 6: Bearing Capacity & Settlement of Foundations

rows: MODES OF SOIL FAILURE

• General Shear: Sudden failure with well-defined shear surfaces; typical in dense sands & stiff clays
• Local Shear: Partial failure; occurs in medium dense soils
• Punching Shear: Small settlement, no distinct shear plane; typical in soft soils
THEORIES OF BEARING CAPACITY

• Terzaghi's Theory (1925) for shallow foundations:
q_ult = c N_c + γ D_f N_q + 0.5 γ B N_y
- c: cohesion, γ: unit weight, D_f: depth, B: width, N_c, N_q, N_y: bearing capacity factors
• Meyerhof, Hansen: Include shape, depth, inclination, water table correction factors
EFFECT OF WATER TABLE

• Reduces effective stress → reduces bearing capacity
• Corrected ultimate bearing: q_ult,water = q_ult,dry - γ_w D_f N_q (if water table at foundation base)
BEARING CAPACITY FROM IN-SITU TESTS

• SPT-based: q_ult ≈ N × factor (depending on soil type)
• Plate Load Test: Measures q_allow directly for shallow foundations
SETTLEMENT

Total settlement = Immediate (elastic) + Primary consolidation + Secondary compression
• Depends on: Soil compressibility, thickness of soil layer, applied load, groundwater conditions
ALLOWABLE BEARING CAPACITY
q_allow = q_ult / FS; FS = Factor of Safety, typically 2.5–3 for shallow foundations

items.name: TERZAGHI BEARING CAPACITY
items.expr: q_ult = c N_c + γ D_f N_q + 0.5 γ B N_y

items.name: SETTLEMENT (ELASTIC)
items.expr: S = (q B (1 - μ²) / E_s) × L_s (B/L ratio factor)

options.text: q_ult = c N_c + γ D_f N_q + 0.5 γ B N_y
q_ult = c + γ D_f + γ B

rows: MAT (RAFT) FOUNDATION

• Large continuous foundation covering the entire structure footprint
• Used when soil bearing capacity is low or column loads are close
• Reduces differential settlement
• Settlement calculated similar to shallow foundation but for entire mat area: S = Σ(H × Δe / (1 + e_0)) over layers
PILE FOUNDATION

• Transfers loads to deeper, stronger soil layers or rock
• Types: End-bearing piles (load carried by tip) & Friction piles (load carried by skin friction)
• Materials: Concrete, steel, timber
• Single pile capacity: Q_p = Q_b + Q_s – Q_b = end-bearing capacity, Q_s = skin friction capacity
• Group efficiency: η_group = Q_group / (Σ Q_single)
NEGATIVE SKIN FRICTION

• Occurs when surrounding soil settles more than pile → downward drag on pile
• Reduces net pile capacity
WELL FOUNDATION

• Used in bridges, piers, or water structures
• Components: Cutting edge, well curb, well curb cap, shaft, well cap
• Types: Cylindrical, Rectangular, Composite wells
• Load transfer: Base bearing + shaft friction
• Depth depends on soil strata & scour allowance

sections.title: Section C: Soil Mechanics & Foundation Engineering – Summary Table

rows: Soil Mechanics - Phase Relations & Index Properties

- Soil as 3-phase system: solids, water, air
- Void ratio: e = V_v/V_s, Porosity: n = V_v/V
- Water content: w = W_w/W_s × 100%
- Atterberg limits: LL, PL, PI = LL - PL
- Soil classification: USCS/IS based on grain size & plasticity

1. Void ratio = V_v/V_s
2. Porosity = V_v/V
3. PI = LL - PL
4. Coarse soil = Gravel & Sand
5. Fine soil = Silt & Clay
Effective Stress, Capillarity & Permeability

- Effective stress: σ' = σ - u
- Darcy's law: Q = k i A
- Capillary rise: h_c = 0.15/d
- Quick sand: i_critical = γ_sat/γ_w - 1
- Constant head test (coarse), Falling head test (fine)

1. Effective stress = σ - u
2. Darcy's law: Q = k i A
3. Quick sand when i ≥ i_critical
4. Capillary rise in fine pores
5. Permeability units: m/s
Compaction, Compressibility & Consolidation

- Compaction increases density, strength
- Proctor test: ρ_dmax & w_opt
- Settlement: S = H × Δe/(1 + e_0)
- Consolidation: time-dependent volume decrease
- Cc for NC soil, Cr for OC soil

1. Compaction increases dry density
2. Optimum moisture → max density
3. Settlement = H × Δe/(1 + e_0)
4. Primary + Secondary consolidation
5. Degree of compaction = (ρ_d,field/ρ_d,max)×100%
Shear Strength & Slope Stability

- Mohr-Coulomb: τ = c + σ tan φ
- Direct shear, Triaxial, Unconfined tests
- FS = Resisting/Driving shear
- Slope failure types: circular, translational
- Groundwater reduces FS

1. Shear strength = c + σ tan φ
2. FS = Resisting/Driving
3. FS > 1 stable, FS < 1 failure
4. Principal stresses: σ₁, σ₃
5. Groundwater reduces stability
Foundation Engineering - Site Investigation & Earth Pressure

- SPT: N-value (blows/30cm)
- Plate load test for q_allow
- Earth pressure: Active, Passive, At-rest
- Rankine: K_a = tan²(45-φ/2), K_p = tan²(45+φ/2)
- Retaining walls: Gravity, Cantilever, Sheet pile

1. SPT measures N-value
2. Active: wall moves away
3. Passive: soil resists wall
4. K_a = tan²(45-φ/2)
5. K_p = tan²(45+φ/2)
Bearing Capacity & Settlement

- Terzaghi: q_ult = cN_c + γD_fN_q + 0.5γBN_y
- q_allow = q_ult/FS (FS=2.5-3)
- Failure modes: General, Local, Punching
- Settlement = Immediate + Primary + Secondary
- Water table reduces capacity

1. Terzaghi: q_ult = cN_c + γD_fN_q + 0.5γBN_y
2. q_allow = q_ult/FS
3. General shear in dense soils
4. Settlement has 3 components
5. Water table reduces bearing capacity
Mat, Pile & Well Foundations

- Mat: for low bearing capacity, reduces differential settlement
- Pile: Q_p = Q_b + Q_s, η_group = Q_group/ΣQ_single
- Negative skin friction reduces capacity
- Well: for bridges/piers, components: cutting edge, shaft, cap

1. Mat reduces differential settlement
2. Pile capacity = end-bearing + skin friction
3. Group efficiency < 1
4. Negative skin friction reduces capacity
5. Well for bridge piers
